segments.s343-g1: This is a nominal phrase in which the adjective شب تیره (literally “night dark”) precedes the proper name شبدیز. The final لهراسپی is an adjectival/nisba form (‑ی) derived from لهراسپ, marking Shabdiz as ‘Lohrasp‑related’ or ‘Lohraspian’ (an epithet). Word order (adj‑noun‑epithet) is literary/poetic but straightforward.
segments.s343-g2: بیاورد is a classical past tense 3rd‑person singular meaning “brought.” The preposition با introduces the accompaniment/instrument (با + object), here زین گشتاسپی: زین “saddle” modified by the adjectival genitive گشتاسپی (“of/related to Gushtasp”). The clause thus means “brought ... with Gushtasp’s saddle.”
segments.s343-g3: بپوشید is past 3rd‑person singular (“he put on / donned”). The sequence of modifiers زربفت رومی precedes the head noun قبای — a common poetic order: “a gold‑brocaded (زربفت), Roman (رومی) robe (قبای).” The verb governs the following noun phrase as its direct object.
segments.s343-g4: ز here is the poetic preposition ‘from/of’; تاج is the crown. اندر آویخت places the verb آویخت (past “hung”) with the locative/in‑preposition اندر (“upon/on”), so literally “hung on the crown.” پر همای is a noun phrase “feather (پر) of the Homa (همای),” with پر as the tangible item and همای the mythical bird name; the whole clause describes something suspended from the crown.
segments.s343-g5: ز is the poetic preposition “from.” دینار is literal coinage/wealth and گوهر is “gem.” شاهوار is an adjective meaning “regal, kingly,” modifying گوهر. The phrase lists the sources/materials of what he brings: “from dinars and from regal gems.”
segments.s343-g6: بیاورد is again past “he brought.” چندان is an adverb “so much/so great an amount.” کش آمد is an idiomatic compound here: آمد is the past “came,” and کش is part of the compound that yields the sense “proved/turned out” or “came into use” (not the literal “pull”); treat the pair as a verbal idiom meaning “proved/useful.” به کار is a prepositional phrase “to/for use.”
segments.s343-g7: از ایران سوی روم is a directional prepositional sequence: از (= from) + ایران (Iran) and سوی (= toward) روم (Rome). بنهاد is past “he set/placed” and, combined with روی (face), gives the idiom بنهاد روی “he turned/set his face,” i.e. he directed himself toward Rome. The verb precedes the object phrase in this literary order.
segments.s343-g8: This clause is compact and poetic: به marks the adverbial location/attitude (“in/with”), دل گاه combines دل (“heart”) + گاه (“time/place/occasion”) to indicate an inward stance or intention, and the remaining words describe the object of that resolve. جوی here is used in the sense “seeking/questing” (not “stream”), روان as “flowing/rapid,” and راه is “road/way”; the final جوی repeats that seeking/questing sense. Together they mean he set his heart on the questing, swift path. The repetition and adjective‑noun order are stylistic.
segments.s343-g9: This is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چون (here functioning like “when/since”). The structure is Subject (پدر) + temporal conjunction (چون) + prepositional/agent phrase ز گشتاسپ + predicate آگاه شد (past verb ‘became aware’). آگاه شد is a compound of the adjective آگاه and the past auxiliary شد meaning “became aware.”
segments.s343-g10: Main clause with two coordinated predicates: بپیچید is a past singular/3rd‑person verb meaning ‘he turned/turned away,’ followed by coordinating و ‘and.’ شادیش is شاد + possessive suffix -ش ‘his/its,’ functioning as a noun ‘his happiness/joy,’ and کوتاه شد is a passive/resultative expression ‘became short / was cut short.’ Word order is subject omitted (understood from previous clause) + verb phrase.
segments.s343-g11: Verb phrase with object list: proper name زریر and the coordinated noun phrase همه بخردان (‘all the wise/wise men’) form the definite object marked by را; بخواند is a perfective/past or historic present verb here meaning ‘he summoned/called together’ (classical usage can mean ‘call’). The object marker را marks the whole preceding phrase as definite direct object.
segments.s343-g12: This clause gives what he did about Gushtasp: ز is the poetic preposition ‘about/of’ or ‘concerning,’ followed by the name گشتاسپ. چندی quantifies سخنها as ‘a few/some words’; براند is the verb (here classical/historic present/past form) meaning ‘uttered/put forth’ (literal lexeme listed as ‘drive,’ but contextually it functions as ‘put forward/say’). Word order places the prepositional agent first for emphasis.
segments.s343-g13: بدیشان is the archaic/poetic form of به ایشان, a prepositional phrase placed before the reporting verb meaning 'to them'. چنین گفت is 'said thus' (چنین 'thus' + گفت, the past tense of گفتن 'to say'). کاین is the literary contraction of که این ('that this') and introduces the reported clause. شیرِ مرد is a compound where شیر ('lion', used metaphorically) qualifies مرد ('man'), i.e. 'a lion-like/valiant man'.
segments.s343-g14: سر تاجدار is a noun phrase with سر 'head' modified by تاجدار 'crowned' (tāj-dār = 'one who bears a crown', i.e. the king's head). اندر آرد is a literary use of آوردن ('to bring'); آرد here is third-person singular (classical/present/future sense 'brings/will bring'), with اندر + verb giving the sense 'bring into/among'. به گرد gives the direction/location 'into the midst/around' (گرد = 'around/circle/midst'). Note the object precedes the verb (typical Persian order) and the whole clause is subordinate to the reporting verb.
segments.s343-g15: چه is the interrogative 'what' and here begins a question. بینید is 2nd-person-plural present/subjunctive/optative form of دیدن used interrogatively ('do you see' / 'do you perceive' / 'what do you foresee'). و links the two clauses. این را uses را as the definite/object marker after این ('this'). چه درمان کنید: درمان is the noun 'remedy/treatment' and کنید is 2nd-person-plural present/subjunctive of کردن used to ask 'what remedy will you do/apply'. Together: 'What do you see, and what remedy will you apply to this?'
segments.s343-g16: نشاید is an impersonal negative 'it is not fitting/it ought not to be' (classical equivalent of نباید). که introduces the subordinate clause. این 'this' is the subject/topic; بر دل 'upon the heart' is a locution meaning 'to the heart' or 'affecting the heart/emotions'. آسان کنید is 2nd-person-plural ('you make [it] easy'), with کنید again subjunctive/optative; the whole clause means 'it is not fitting that you make this easy on the heart' (= 'you must not take this lightly').
segments.s343-g33: The verb فرستاد (sent) precedes the subject لهراسپ — a common classical/Persian inverted order: 'فرستاد لهراسپ چندی مهان' = 'Lohrasp sent several guests.' چندی is an indefinite quantifier modifying مهان (plural 'guests').
segments.s343-g34: به + infinitive (به جستن) plus گرفتن (گرفتند) is an idiom meaning 'to take to doing / begin to do' — here 'they began to search.' گرفتند is past plural 'they took/started.' گرد جهان is an adverbial phrase 'around the world.'
segments.s343-g35: برفتند and بازآمدند are coordinated past-plural verbs 'they went and they returned.' نومید (an adjective) is placed before بازآمدند and functions as a predicative/adverbial modifier: 'they returned hopeless' or 'they came back in despair.'
segments.s343-g36: که is used causally here ('because'); 'با اختر دیرساز آمدند' literally 'they came with a delaying star' — an idiom meaning they came under the influence/omen of a (ill) star. دیرساز is an attributive noun 'delayer' modifying اختر ('star').
segments.s343-g37: نکوهش 'reproach/blame' is the subject phrase; از آن 'concerning that' (از as 'of/concerning') specifies what the reproach is about. بهر marks the target/recipient ('for/to') and بود is the past copula: 'the reproach for that was for Lohrasp' = 'the blame for that fell on Lohrasp.'
segments.s343-g38: غم و رنج تن is a coordinated noun phrase 'sorrow and bodily suffering' (تن qualifies رنج as bodily suffering). بهر گشتاسپ بود uses بهر to mark the one who bears these, and بود is the past copula: 'were for Gushtasp' = 'fell upon Gushtasp.'
segments.s343-g17: چنین گفت موبد که = “thus said the mobad that…”: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so,” گفت is the reporting verb (classical past ‘said’) and موبد is the speaker (the priest). که is the complementizer introducing the subordinate clause that follows. Word order places the adverb + verb + speaker before که, a normal poetic arrangement for reported speech.
segments.s343-g18: گرامی به مردان بود تاج و تخت = “the crown and throne were dear to men”: بود is the copula in past tense, here following its predicate phrase (گرامی به مردان) and preceding the concrete nouns (تاج و تخت). به + مردان marks the experiencer/beneficiary (‘to/for men’). The nouns تاج و تخت appear at the end as the entities described by the predicate.
segments.s343-g19: چو گشتاسپ فرزند کس را نبود = “when Gushtasp had no child”: چو is the temporal conjunction “when,” followed by the subject name گشتاسپ and the noun فرزند. کس را نبود is an existential negative construction — کس here means “no one” and را is the (old/poetic) object/topic marker; نبود is the past copula/negation ‘there was not’. The clause as a whole sets the time/condition for the next line.
segments.s343-g20: نه هرگز کس از نامداران شنود = “never did any of the renowned hear (of one)”: نه هرگز is an emphatic negative adverbial phrase ‘never/at no time,’ کس is the indefinite/negative subject ‘anyone/no one,’ از + نامداران means ‘of/among the renowned,’ and شنود is a third-person verb (historic/presentive) ‘hears/heard.’ The verb comes at the end, typical of Persian clause order.
segments.s343-g21: ز هر سو بباید فرستاد کس = “from every side one must send someone”: ز هر سو is a fronted prepositional phrase ‘from every direction/side.’ بباید is the modal ‘one must/ought to,’ followed by the infinitive/past-stem فرستاد ‘send’ (classical structure بباید + verb). کس is an indefinite pronoun ‘someone’ that will be specified by the following noun phrase.
segments.s343-g22: دلاور بزرگان فریادرس = “a valiant rescuer of the great ones”: this is an appositive noun phrase describing کس. دلاور is an epithet ‘valiant,’ بزرگان is a genitive/possessive plural ‘of the great/elders/nobles,’ and فریادرس is the head noun ‘rescuer’ (one who comes to aid). In Persian poetry the linking -ِ (ezāfe) may be omitted in writing but the relationship is genitive: فریادرسِ بزرگان.
segments.s343-g23: گر او بازگردد تو زفتی مکن = “if he returns, do not tarry”: گر introduces a condition ‘if,’ او بازگردد is the protasis ‘he returns’ (بازگردد is the classical present/subjunctive or historic-present of ‘return’), and the main clause is negative imperative formed with تو … مکن ‘you do not …’. زفتی is an archaic verb/adverb ‘to tarry/delay’; here it is the action prohibited by مکن.
segments.s343-g24: هنرجوی و با آز جفتی مکن = “do not be an art‑seeker, and do not pair with greed”: هنرجوی functions as a predicate/noun phrase ‘(do) not be an art‑seeker/apprentice’ governed by the negative imperative مکن; the و links the second prohibition, با + آز = ‘with greed,’ and جفتی مکن ‘do not pair/mate’ forbids allying or coupling with greed. The negative verb مکن applies to both coordinated directives.
segments.s343-g25: که introduces a subordinate clause ('when/that'). تاج کیان is a genitival phrase 'the crown of the Kians' (tāj + Kiyān). چون تو is a comparative phrase 'like you' placed before the verb by poetic word order. بیند is 3rd person singular present of دیدن ('sees' / 'beholds') — in classical Persian present-tense forms are common in narrative. بسی is an adverb of degree ('much/enough') placed after the verb for metrical reasons and will be understood as modifying the following predicate.
segments.s343-g26: نماند is the (present) verb 'remain' (here with negative implication in the whole phrase); همی is an older/pervasive adverb (like medieval می) giving continuity/emphasis ('still/indeed') and is common in poetic diction. مهر او is the direct noun phrase 'his/its love, favour', with او as the possessor; بر کسی means 'upon/for anyone' — in a clause with نماند the sense is 'will not remain for anyone'. Word order places the verb earlier and its object phrases after for meter and emphasis.
segments.s343-g27: به before a personal name marks the indirect object 'to/for Gushtasp' (classical Persian often uses به + name to mark the recipient). زین here functions as a classical compressed form meaning 'of/from this' (poetic contraction of از این) and combines with جهان 'world' and کشوری 'realm(s)' to form the noun phrase 'ten realms of this world' (the numeral 'ده' appears in the line but is not in the listed words here). The order is somewhat inverted for meter: recipient (به گشتاسپ) comes first, then the domain phrase.
segments.s343-g28: بنه is an imperative-like verb here meaning 'place/put' (an archaic/poetic command from نهادن/بهادن); it begins the second half of the sentence. بر سرش = 'upon his head' (بر + سر + the enclitic ش = 'his'). نامدار is an adjective 'renowned', modifying افسری ('a diadem/helmet/diademic crown'). The verb governs the following prepositional phrase and noun phrase; the order (verb — preposition — possessor — epithet — noun) is poetic.
segments.s343-g29: جز از is a prepositional/exclusive phrase 'except (from)'; it excludes the following noun from the generalization. پهلوان رستم نامدار is a noun phrase 'the champion Rostam, renowned' with the epithet نامدار after the name — placing adjectives after the noun is common in Persian poetry for emphasis and meter.
segments.s343-g30: به گیتی = 'in the world' (the preposition به + noun), setting the domain. نبینیم is 1st person plural present negative 'we do not see' (the negative prefix n- attached to the present form), used here as a generalizing statement. چون او سوار = 'a rider like him' (چون = 'like/as', او = 'he', سوار = 'rider/horseman'); the comparative phrase follows the verb for poetic word order, so the clause means 'we see no rider like him in the world.'
segments.s343-g31: به here introduces the list of qualities and works like 'in' or 'with regard to' (classical uses of به are flexible). The four nouns are linked by و ('and'): بالا 'height/loftiness', دیدار 'appearance/visage or meeting' (here 'appearance/presence'), فرهنگ 'culture/refinement' and هوش 'intellect/wit'. This is a stacked attribute list describing the domain in which the following claim holds.
segments.s343-g32: چنو (چنان) means 'such/as' and introduces the comparative 'so famed'. نامور is 'renowned'. نیز 'also/likewise' here functions as an adverb often used in negative or emphatic clauses. نشنید is past tense 'did not hear' or 'has never heard' (classical poetry often uses past to state a timeless negative), and گوش 'ear' is the experiencer — 'the ear has heard of none so famed.' Overall, the clause asserts that nobody so famous has been heard of in those qualities.

sentences.s343-s1: The dark night brought Shabdiz, the Lohraspian, with Gushtasp's saddle.
sentences.s343-s2: He donned a Roman gold‑brocaded robe; from his crown hung a Homa feather.
sentences.s343-s3: From dinars and regal gems he brought so much that it proved useful.
sentences.s343-s4: From Iran he turned his face toward Rome; in his heart he set about the seeking, flowing road.
sentences.s343-s5: When the father became aware of Gushtasp, he turned aside and his joy was cut short.
sentences.s343-s6: He summoned Zarir and all the wise men, and said a few words about Gushtasp.
sentences.s343-s7: He said to them thus: that this lion-like man will bring the crowned head into the midst.
sentences.s343-s8: What do you see, and what remedy will you apply to this? It is not fitting that you make this easy on the heart.
sentences.s343-s17: Lohrasp sent several guests; they set out to search the world.
sentences.s343-s18: They went and returned hopeless, for they had come under a delaying star.
sentences.s343-s19: The blame for that fell on Lohrasp; the sorrow and bodily suffering fell on Gushtasp.
sentences.s343-s9: Thus the mobad said that this fortunate one held crown and throne dear among men.
sentences.s343-s10: When Gushtasp had no son, never did any of the renowned hear (of one).
sentences.s343-s11: From every direction one must send someone — a valiant rescuer of the great (men).
sentences.s343-s12: If he returns, do not delay; do not play the art‑seeker, and do not pair with greed.
sentences.s343-s13: When the crown of the Kians beholds someone like you, its favor will remain for no one.
sentences.s343-s14: Give Gushtasp ten realms of this world; place upon his head a famed diadem.
sentences.s343-s15: Except for the renowned champion Rostam, we see no rider like him in the world.
sentences.s343-s16: In loftiness, appearance, refinement and intellect, the ear has heard of none so famed.
